planting.note: Start seeds indoors 10–12 weeks before last frost; germination is slow and erratic, sometimes taking up to 6 weeks — bottom heat at 80°F and light scarification improve rates. Transplant after all frost danger passes in full sun; in zones 8b–11 established plants may re-sprout perennially from the base if mulched heavily.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile soil; the chiltepin is the only wild chile pepper native to the United States, growing in rocky canyon bottoms and shaded washes from Texas through Arizona and south into Mexico. The tiny round to oval fruits — barely the size of a pea — ripen from green to vivid red and pack an intense, clean heat (50,000–100,000 Scoville) that fades quickly unlike cultivated chiles. Birds disperse the seeds and are unaffected by capsaicin, making this a superb wildlife plant as well as a culinary one. In mild winter zones (8b+) plants are truly perennial and can reach shrub size over several seasons; in colder climates grow as an annual started indoors. Harvest fruits as they redden or allow birds to disperse naturally for a self-seeding colony.
sources: Bosland & Votava: Peppers: Vegetable and Spice Capsicums, CABI 2012

common_name: Crowder Pea (Iron and Clay, Mississippi Purple)

planting.note: Direct sow 1 inch deep after soil warms to at least 65°F; crowder peas are extremely heat-tolerant and thrive in the deep South when planted through summer — successive plantings every 3–4 weeks extend the harvest season through first fall frost.

directions: Sow seeds 1 inch deep in rows 18–24 inches apart in full sun in warm, well-drained, moderately fertile soil; crowder peas get their name because the seeds are crowded so tightly in the pod that they press against each other and develop flat sides. They are a cornerstone of Southern cuisine — shelled fresh and cooked with pot likker and fatback, or dried for winter storage — and also a superb summer cover crop that fixes atmospheric nitrogen at high rates in hot weather. Bush-type plants need no support and mature quickly even in poor, sandy soils. Pick pods when they have turned fully colored (purple, tan, or buff depending on variety) and the seeds feel firm but not hard for fresh-shell use; allow pods to dry completely on the vine for dry peas.
sources: Southern Exposure Seed Exchange cultural notes

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant after frost danger passes and soil has warmed above 60°F. In the Gulf Coast (zone 9), plant in late February to March for a spring crop and again in late August for a fall crop — the plants struggle in peak summer heat above 95°F.

directions: Plant in full sun in fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic soil (pH 6.0–6.8) with consistent moisture; grape tomatoes are distinguished by their small, elongated, thick-walled fruits — typically 1–2 inches long and grape-shaped — with a sweeter, less acidic flavor and firmer texture than cherry tomatoes, making them excellent for eating fresh, roasting, and salads. Most varieties are indeterminate and highly productive, setting fruit prolifically in large pendant clusters throughout the growing season. The thick skin resists cracking even when watering is inconsistent, a significant advantage over round cherry types. Provide a sturdy cage or stake early, keep soil evenly moist to prevent blossom-end rot, and side-dress with balanced fertilizer when the first flowers open.

common_name: Land Cress (Upland Cress, American Cress, Early Yellow Rocket)

planting.note: Direct sow as early as soil can be worked in spring, 4–6 weeks before last frost; make a second sowing 6–8 weeks before first fall frost for the main harvest season — the flavor improves markedly after light frost exposure. Land cress overwinters as a rosette in zones 6–9 when sown in fall.

directions: Sow seeds thinly 1/4 inch deep in rows or broadcast in beds; land cress is a practical substitute for watercress that grows on ordinary garden soil without the need for flowing water. The dark, glossy, pinnate leaves have a pungent peppery flavor similar to watercress — making them excellent in salads, sandwiches, and as a garnish — but the plant is far more adaptable, tolerating drier conditions and surviving light frost. Flavor sharpens after frost exposure in fall and winter. Harvest outer leaves continuously from 50 days onward or cut whole rosettes. Plants bolt to flower in warm weather, producing edible yellow flowers; re-sow for the next season before temperatures exceed 70°F. Partial shade in warm climates extends the harvest and reduces bitterness.

planting.note: For the traditional Italian winter harvest, direct sow in late summer (late July to August in zone 9) so heads form during cool weather; puntarelle is a cool-season biennial that bolts quickly in warm weather — its distinctive hollow asparagus-like shoots only form after the plant experiences cool autumn temperatures.

directions: Sow seeds 1/4 inch deep and thin to 18–24 inches; puntarelle is a treasured Italian winter vegetable from the Lazio region, prized for its hollow, asparagus-like inner shoots that emerge from the center of the large chicory head. After harvest, the shoots are shaved or sliced thin, soaked in cold water to curl and reduce bitterness, and served raw in the classic Roman preparation with anchovy-garlic-lemon dressing. The outer leaves are also edible and mildly bitter in the chicory style. Sow in late summer for fall and winter harvest — in zone 8–10 this is an excellent late-season crop that matures when few other vegetables are producing. The plant is a biennial that sends up a tall flower stalk in its second spring; grow as an annual for the best eating shoots.
sources: Slow Food International Ark of Taste
Orto Botanico di Roma research notes
University of Gastronomic Sciences — Italian Heritage Vegetables

planting.note: Direct sow 1–2 inches deep after last frost when soil is warm above 65°F; Whippoorwill is one of the most drought-tolerant and heat-tolerant of the Southern peas and performs well even when planted in poor, sandy soil with little supplemental fertilizer — excess nitrogen from fertilizing reduces pod production in all cowpea types.

directions: Sow seeds 1–2 inches deep in any well-drained soil in full sun after last frost; Whippoorwill is one of the oldest and most historically documented American cowpea varieties, named for the distinctive brown mottling on its tan seeds that supposedly resembles the speckled breast of the whippoorwill bird. It is a traditional field pea of the South, used for fresh shelling (green stage), dried beans, and as a nitrogen-fixing cover crop, and is notably tolerant of the heat, drought, and poor sandy soils of the Gulf Coast and Deep South where other legumes struggle. The plants are vigorous, bushy, prolific, and essentially maintenance-free once established — genuine drought tolerance means established plants can survive extended dry periods that would kill modern beans. For fresh eating, shell when pods are full and green; for dry beans, allow pods to brown and dry on the plant before harvest. An outstanding edible cover crop that improves soil while producing food.

planting.note: Direct sow 1–2 inches deep after last frost when soil is warm; Zipper Cream is adapted to Gulf Coast and Deep South conditions and performs well in the summer heat. Multiple plantings 3–4 weeks apart extend the harvest season; in zone 9 plant from April through August for successive harvests into fall.

directions: Sow seeds 1–2 inches deep in any well-drained soil in full sun; Zipper Cream is one of the most popular Southern peas in home gardens and small farms of the Gulf Coast for its exceptionally easy shelling — the pods split along a natural 'zipper' seam that allows the cream-colored peas to fall out with almost no effort, a practical advantage that makes large harvests much faster to process. The peas themselves are plump, cream-white, and have a mild, sweet, buttery flavor prized in Southern pea dishes, boiled fresh with ham or bacon, or frozen for winter use. A bush-type plant that produces heavily over a long season; heat and drought tolerant once established. One of the most recommended varieties for first-time Southern pea growers because of the easy harvest and reliable production.

planting.note: Direct sow 1–1.5 inches deep in warm soil after last frost; Lady pea is one of the smallest of the Southern peas with very small seeds, so somewhat more of them are required per planting. It is heat tolerant and produces reliably in zone 8–10 Gulf Coast summers.

directions: Sow seeds 1–1.5 inches deep in any well-drained, lean soil in full sun after last frost; Lady cream pea is a traditional Southern heirloom cowpea producing small, white, round peas with a notably sweet, mild, delicate flavor — milder and less earthy than crowder peas or black-eyed peas, and considered by many Southern pea aficionados to have the finest flavor of all the cream peas for fresh eating and freezing. The small size and mild sweetness make it the variety most often served to guests unfamiliar with Southern peas, as the flavor is more approachable and universally liked. A true bush-type plant that is drought-tolerant once established; performs reliably in the heat and humidity of the Gulf Coast. Excellent boiled simply with butter and salt and served as a side dish, or incorporated into succotash, hoppin' John, and traditional Southern vegetable plates.

planting.note: For fall planting in zone 9, start transplants in late August–September and set out October–November; in the Gulf South, collards are primarily a fall through spring vegetable — the mild winters allow nearly year-round harvests. In zone 9 summer heat, plants often go to seed; replant fresh transplants each fall for the best quality greens.

directions: Set transplants 18–24 inches apart in fertile, well-drained soil; in zone 9 plant fall transplants October–November for harvest December through March; Vates is the classic Southern collard variety developed at the Virginia Truck Experiment Station in the 1950s and for decades was the standard garden collard of the South — compact, slow to bolt, very cold hardy (surviving temperatures well below 20°F with frost actually improving the flavor by converting starches to sugars), and producing thick, blue-green leaves of the authentic Southern collard flavor. The name 'Vates' (meaning 'prophet' in Latin) was chosen by the Virginia researchers. Harvest outer leaves regularly from the bottom of the plant upward, leaving the growing tip to continue producing; plants that are allowed to keep their lower leaves become leggy and produce smaller, tougher leaves. The slow-bolt characteristic makes Vates substantially more useful in cool-season gardens than older varieties that rush to flower.

common_name: White Half Runner Bean (Mountaineer Half Runner)

planting.note: Direct sow 1–2 inches deep after last frost in any fertile, well-drained soil; white half runner needs only 2–3 feet of support — short stakes or a low fence is sufficient for the semi-vining habit. Successive plantings every 2–3 weeks extend the harvest through the season.

directions: Sow seeds 1–2 inches deep in well-drained, fertile soil in full sun, providing 2–3 feet of support for the semi-vining plants; white half runner is the most popular snap bean in the home gardens and farmers markets of the Appalachian mountains and Southern uplands, occupying the productive middle ground between bush beans (no support needed, shorter production) and full pole beans (requires tall trellis, longer season). The semi-vining plants produce abundantly over a concentrated 2–3 week period, making them excellent for canning and freezing in large batches as well as fresh table use. The pods are round, stringless when young, and have excellent fresh-eating flavor. White seeds make it useful as a dry bean after pods mature fully. The Mountaineer White Half Runner was bred at the West Virginia Extension station specifically for the cool-summer Appalachian region but is equally productive in the Gulf South when planted in spring or fall.

common_name: October Bean (Peanut Butter Bean, Speckled Pole Bean)

planting.note: Direct sow 1–2 inches deep after last frost; October bean is a pole or half-runner type requiring 4–6 foot support; in zone 9 plant in May–June to mature in September–October when cooler temperatures improve the flavor of the shelled beans.

directions: Sow seeds 1–2 inches deep in fertile, well-drained soil in full sun, providing 4–6 feet of vertical support; October bean is a traditional Appalachian heirloom pole or half-runner bean whose primary value is as a shelling bean — harvested when pods are fully filled but still green to slightly dried ('fall colored'), when the speckled tan-and-maroon seeds shell out easily and cook quickly with a rich, creamy, almost peanut-buttery flavor. Named for October when they traditionally matured in the mountain regions of the southern Appalachians, the plants produce best when planted in late spring to mature into cooler fall temperatures. In zone 9 a June planting produces a September–October shell bean harvest with ideal timing. The shelled beans cook in 45–60 minutes without soaking, absorbing seasoning beautifully, and are outstanding cooked with ham hocks and served over cornbread.
